Some purchase requests moved through three approval levels while others required six. Certain business units consistently bypassed automated workflows and relied on manual interventions. Lead times varied considerably despite similar business requirements.

Without process mining, these variations would likely have remained hidden until much later in the transformation program.

By identifying these differences early, the organization was able to simplify approval paths, reduce unnecessary complexity, and establish more consistent operating procedures.

Illustrative Procurement Process Variations Identified Through SAP Signavio

Illustrative example showing how SAP Signavio process mining can identify approval bottlenecks, process variations, and manual workarounds across regions. Sample process flows are provided for educational purposes only.

Why Process Standardization Matters

One of the primary goals of many SAP S/4HANA programs is process standardization.

Organizations often seek to:

  • Improve efficiency

  • Reduce operational risk

  • Increase compliance

  • Simplify support models

  • Improve reporting consistency

  • Enable scalable growth

However, standardization becomes difficult when the organization lacks visibility into existing process variations.

SAP Signavio helps establish that visibility.

By understanding how work actually flows through the business, organizations can make informed decisions about which processes should be standardized and where flexibility remains necessary.

Supporting Successful S/4HANA Transformations

Many organizations view process mining as a separate initiative.

I believe its greatest value comes when it is integrated directly into SAP transformation programs.

When used early in the project lifecycle, SAP Signavio can help teams:

  • Establish process baselines

  • Identify improvement opportunities

  • Reduce process complexity

  • Improve business readiness

  • Support change management activities

  • Increase confidence in future-state designs

This reduces the risk of discovering major process issues during testing or after go-live.

SAP Signavio Supporting SAP S4HANA Transformation Programs

Illustrative example showing how SAP Signavio process mining supports SAP S/4HANA transformation programs by identifying bottlenecks, enabling process optimization, improving compliance, and driving better business outcomes.

Beyond Technology

One misconception is that SAP transformations fail because of technology limitations.

In reality, many transformation challenges originate from process inconsistencies, unclear ownership, or operational inefficiencies that existed long before the project began.

Technology can enable improvement, but it cannot automatically correct broken processes.

Tools such as SAP Signavio help organizations understand those processes before large-scale changes are introduced.

That understanding often becomes one of the most valuable outcomes of the transformation itself.

Conclusion

From my experience, some of the most important discoveries in SAP transformation programs come from understanding how the business actually operates rather than how it believes it operates.

SAP Signavio provides organizations with a data-driven approach to process visibility, helping uncover bottlenecks, variations, and inefficiencies that traditional assessments may overlook.

As organizations continue investing in SAP S/4HANA and digital transformation initiatives, process transparency will become increasingly important. The earlier process issues are identified, the easier they are to address, and the greater the likelihood of achieving sustainable transformation outcomes.

entry.title: Five Mistakes Organizations Make During SAP S/4HANA Data Migration and How to Avoid Them
entry.content:
Five Mistakes Organizations Make During SAP S/4HANA Data Migration and How to Avoid Them

Introduction

Data migration is often one of the most challenging components of an SAP S/4HANA transformation. While organizations invest significant effort in system design, testing, and cutover planning, data migration frequently becomes a source of delays, quality issues, and post-go-live support challenges.

Over the years, I have participated in multiple SAP transformation programs where data migration played a critical role in overall project success. Although every program is different, I have consistently observed a few common mistakes that increase migration risk and impact business readiness.

This article shares five lessons learned from SAP transformation programs and practical approaches organizations can use to improve migration outcomes.

Mistake #1: Starting Data Cleansing Too Late

One of the most common mistakes is treating data cleansing as a technical activity that can wait until migration development begins.

In reality, data quality issues often exist long before a transformation starts.

Common challenges include:

  • Duplicate records

  • Incomplete master data

  • Inconsistent naming conventions

  • Obsolete business partners

  • Missing attributes

Organizations that begin data cleansing early typically experience smoother testing cycles and fewer migration defects.

How to Avoid It

Start data profiling and data quality assessments as early as possible. Treat data cleansing as a business responsibility supported by the project team rather than a purely technical exercise.

Mistake #2: Underestimating Business Participation

Successful data migration requires active business involvement.

Project teams often assume that technical resources can manage migration activities independently. However, business users play a critical role in validating data quality, reviewing mapping rules, and approving migrated information.

Without business participation, migration defects frequently remain unresolved until later testing phases.

How to Avoid It

Establish clear business ownership for critical data domains and involve business stakeholders throughout migration planning, validation, and testing activities.

Mistake #3: Focusing Only on Data Extraction

Many migration programs concentrate heavily on extraction and loading activities while paying less attention to transformation logic.

Migration success depends not only on moving data but also on ensuring data is structured correctly for the target SAP S/4HANA environment.

Examples include:

  • Business Partner conversion

  • Material master harmonization

  • Chart of accounts alignment

  • Organizational structure mapping

Errors in transformation logic can create significant downstream issues.

How to Avoid It

Invest time in defining transformation rules, mapping strategies, and validation procedures before migration development begins.

Mistake #4: Treating Migration Testing as a One-Time Event

Migration testing should not be limited to a single cycle.

Organizations often perform one migration test, identify issues, and assume the process is ready for production.

In practice, multiple migration rehearsals are usually required to improve quality, refine timing estimates, and validate cutover procedures.

How to Avoid It

Conduct multiple mock migrations and use each cycle to improve performance, data quality, reconciliation accuracy, and operational readiness.

Mistake #5: Measuring Success Only by Load Completion

Completing a data load does not automatically mean the migration was successful.

The real measure of success includes:

  • Data accuracy

  • Reconciliation results

  • Business validation

  • Process execution

  • Reporting accuracy

Organizations that focus only on technical completion may overlook business-critical issues.

How to Avoid It

Establish clear migration success criteria that include both technical and business validation metrics.

The Importance of Migration Governance

Strong governance is essential throughout the migration lifecycle.

Effective governance includes:

  • Data ownership

  • Issue management

  • Validation checkpoints

  • Readiness reviews

  • Executive visibility

Governance helps organizations identify risks early and maintain alignment across business and technical teams.

Looking Ahead: The Role of AI in Data Migration

Artificial intelligence is creating new opportunities to improve migration quality and efficiency.

AI can help organizations:

  • Detect data anomalies

  • Identify duplicate records

  • Predict migration risks

  • Improve reconciliation processes

  • Monitor data quality trends

While AI is not a replacement for business expertise, it can provide valuable insights that improve migration outcomes.

Final Thoughts

Data migration is much more than a technical exercise. It is a business-critical activity that directly impacts user adoption, operational stability, and transformation success.

From my experience, organizations that begin early, engage business stakeholders, establish strong governance, and continuously validate data quality are significantly more likely to achieve successful SAP S/4HANA migration outcomes.

Avoiding these common mistakes can help reduce risk, improve readiness, and increase confidence throughout the transformation journey.

entry.title: Architecting for Autonomy: SAP Sapphire Madrid Takeaways
entry.content:
The SAP Autonomous Enterprise is an AI-driven operating model where AI agents and assistants execute end-to-end business workflows independently. Humans set the strategic direction while AI manages mission-critical processes within built-in governance, security, and compliance guardrails.

This was the strategic theme in the keynote of SAP Sapphire Madrid 2026 - a paradigm shift from Digital transformation to something more ambitious - Autonomous Enterprise.

 

The first step to achieving this ambition, as per SAP, is to lay the groundwork with Business Data Fabric combined with Knowledge Graphs and Company memory, thus establishing a strong foundation of governance and data with context and relation.

This foundation of contextual intelligence is further strengthened by adhering to Clean Core principles with BTP extensibility capabilities.

How can organisations achieve this ambitious goal?

 

Establish "Clean Core with BTP extensibility" as an architecture guideline

Organisations can reap the benefits of moving to S/4 HANA by adopting out-of-the-box features for standard processes and adapting specific requirements using BTP extensibility. This serves as a critical criteria to form a strong data foundation layer. This data foundation can then be enhanced with data from other processes and non-SAP sources through the SAP Business Data Fabric

From Data to Intelligence

Data can be only as useful as the context and relation it has to the processes of the organisation. SAP Knowledge Graphs help in forming these connections between business processes and datasets. This is further aided by Company Memory which captures historical decisions and patterns

Process + AI

Organisations can benefit from migrating from legacy business processes and moving to best practices. Gaps in process can be identified by using process mining tools complimented with SAP Signavio to close these gaps.

After establishing a lean process, SAP recommends using SAP Business AI and Joule to enable intelligent automation across functions such as HR, finance, procurement etc.

Governance Frameworks

One of the key steps to achieving this ambition is to setup a Governance framework where experts define and implement ethics, guardrails and policies on the outcomes of these AI solutions

The journey to transforming into an Autonomous Enterprise is a tough but attainable goal powered by leadership support.

entry.title: Improving SAP HANA DB Performance: Two Common CDS View Pitfalls and How to Fix Them
entry.content:
As application landscapes grow and user adoption increases, performance issues in custom CDS (Core Data Services) views can emerge in ways that are not immediately obvious.
Two patterns in particular are responsible for a significant share of unexpected CPU spikes in production HANA systems:

  1. CDS views using locale-based text retrieval that silently bypass HANA's most efficient execution engine.
  2. ABAP programs passing large filter lists to CDS views, generating thousands of SQL filter conditions at runtime.

Both patterns are common in custom development, both are easy to miss during development and testing, and both have clean, well-documented solutions. This post walks through each one in detail.

The technical foundation of this post is based on knowledge shared by @jayaprakash_subramanian .
His willingness to share his expertise on HANA execution internals and CDS optimization made this post possible - thank you.

Contents

  1. Step 0 — Diagnose Before You Fix
  2. Fix #1 — CDS Views with Locale Parameters Bypassing the HEX Engine
  3. Fix #2 — Large IN-List Conditions from ABAP Range Tables
  4. General Best Practices
  5. Summary
  6. References

Step 0: Before You Fix — Diagnose First

Before applying any optimization, confirm which queries are causing elevated CPU usage.
The SQL Plan Cache in SAP HANA is the primary tool for this.

Check the Execution Engine of a Specific Query

SELECT EXECUTION_ENGINE, STATEMENT_HASH, STATEMENT_STRING
FROM M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE
WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

The EXECUTION_ENGINE column reveals which engine processed the query:

ValueMeaning
HEXHigh-Performance Analytical Engine — optimal for CDS views
COLUMNColumn Engine — less optimized, higher CPU for CDS workloads
ROWRow Engine — used for row-store tables, not typical for CDS views

If you see COLUMN where you expect HEX, the query is not running at its full potential.

Monitor CPU and Memory at the System Level

SAP HANA Cockpit and HANA Studio both provide dashboards for CPU and memory usage over configurable time windows.
Key indicators to watch:

  • CPU usage spikes above 70–80% during business hours, correlated with Fiori app usage.
  • Out-of-Memory (OOM) dumps in HANA trace files or HANA Cockpit - these indicate memory pressure from specific statements.
  • Statement Memory Limit - if individual SQL statements approach the configured limit, they may be terminated or cause cascading failures.
Tip: Always capture both a 7-day and a 24-hour view to distinguish between sustained load and isolated spikes.
This distinction determines whether a fix is urgent or can be planned during a maintenance window.

CDS Views with Locale Parameters Bypassing the HEX Engine

SAP Note 3513507  ·  SAP Note 3076534

Background: How SAP HANA Handles Multilingual Text

SAP systems are designed to operate in multiple languages.
Many business entities - such as product descriptions, unit of measure texts, and customer category labels - are stored in dedicated text tables: separate database tables containing one row per language per entity.


Entity KeyLanguage KeyDescription
ITEM-001ENSteel Pipe
ITEM-001DEStahlrohr
ITEM-001HEצינור פלדה
ITEM-001JA鋼管

When a user opens a Fiori application with their session language set to Hebrew (he), the system must return the Hebrew text row and render it correctly in right-to-left script.
The same mechanism applies to any language that differs from the system installation default.

What WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) Does

In SAP HANA CDS, text tables are modeled using the @ObjectModel.text.association annotation.
When a Fiori application reads a CDS view backed by such an association, it automatically appends a WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) clause to the generated SQL query, passing the current user's session locale as the parameter value.

SELECT *
FROM MY_CDS_VIEW
WITH PARAMETERS ('LOCALE' = 'he')

This instructs HANA to join the text table and return only the rows matching the Hebrew language key.
Without this parameter, the CDS view would either return texts for all languages or fall back to a system default, neither of which is appropriate for a user-facing Fiori application.

Example — Hebrew locale in action

A product or entity search CDS view with LOCALE = 'he' returns Hebrew descriptions:

KeyDescription
ITEM-001צינור פלדה (Steel Pipe)
ITEM-002ספק ראשי בע"מ (Main Supplier Ltd.)
ITEM-003חברת דוגמה בע"מ (Example Company Ltd.)

The Problem: HEX Engine Limitation

SAP HANA's HEX (High-Performance Analytical Execution) Engine is the modern columnar execution engine optimized for CDS views accessed from Fiori applications.
It delivers substantially lower CPU consumption compared to the fallback Column Engine, particularly for queries involving joins, projections, and filters typical of CDS-based reads.

However, as documented in SAP Note 3513507, the HEX engine has a known limitation: it cannot process queries containing a WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) clause.
When such a query arrives, HANA silently falls back to the Column Engine.

Why this goes undetected: This fallback is entirely invisible during development and unit testing.
Results are correct - just processed less efficiently.
The impact only becomes apparent in production, where many users simultaneously access the same Fiori apps in a non-English locale.

This is particularly relevant in any deployment where the working language differs from the system installation default - including Hebrew, Arabic, Japanese, Chinese, Thai, and others.

The Solution: Adding the HEX Hint

There are two approaches, and the correct choice depends on whether you own the CDS view or not.
 
Prerequisite — must be applied before either option below
By default, SAP HANA excludes queries containing `WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?)` from HEX engine execution. Setting 'hex_enable_with_parameters_locale = true' in 'indexserver.ini' removes this restriction at the system level - without it, any hint applied at the view or statement level will have no effect on LOCALE queries.

Requires SAP HANA Revision >= 075 (SPS07).
Do not apply on SAP HANA <= 2.00.074 — see SAP Note 3513507.
ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('indexserver.ini', 'SYSTEM')
SET ('sql','hex_enable_with_parameters_locale') = 'true'
WITH RECONFIGURE COMMENT 'SAP Note 3513507';

ALTER SYSTEM CLEAR SQL PLAN CACHE;

Option 1 — Preferred: Add the Hint Directly in the CDS View

If the CDS view is a custom view that you own and can modify, the preferred approach is to embed the DB hint directly in the CDS view definition using a consumption annotation. This is the clean, transport-safe, source-controlled solution described in SAP Note 2717265.
In the CDS view DDL, add the hint annotation:
@DatabaseHint: [{dbSystem: #HDB, hint: 'USE_HEX_PLAN'}]
define view entity YOUR_CDS_VIEW ...​
Or in the ABAP SELECT consuming the view, use the %_HINTS addition:
SELECT *
  FROM your_cds_view
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
  %_HINTS HDB 'USE_HEX_PLAN'.
This approach keeps the optimization explicit, versioned, and visible in the development layer.

Option 2 — Fallback: ANNOTATION Hint at the HANA Layer

If the CDS view is a standard SAP-delivered view that you cannot modify, or if you need a temporary fix while a proper code change is being prepared, you can apply the hint at the HANA database layer using the ANNOTATION mechanism (SAP Note 3076534).
 
Important: This should not be the first choice. It is a workaround - applied at the database layer outside the ABAP development lifecycle.
Use it only when Option 1 is not applicable.
 
-- Apply the hint
ANNOTATE "YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME" SET 'HINT' = 'USE_HEX_PLAN';

-- Confirm the annotation is registered
SELECT * FROM ANNOTATIONS;

-- Clear the SQL Plan Cache so HANA generates a fresh execution plan
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

To roll back:

ANNOTATE "YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME" UNSET 'HINT';

Regardless of which option is used, verify the result the same way:

SELECT EXECUTION_ENGINE, STATEMENT_HASH
FROM M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE
WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

The EXECUTION_ENGINE column should now show HEX.

Large IN-List Conditions from ABAP Range Tables

SAP Note 2399993 -  SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)

The Problem: How ABAP Generates Thousands of SQL Filter Conditions

A common ABAP pattern for reading data from a CDS view is to build a range table and pass it using the IN operator:

SELECT *
FROM zcds_my_view
INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
WHERE key_field IN @LR_range.

This is clean, readable ABAP - but it has a critical hidden cost at the database layer.
When ABAP's DB interface translates this statement into SQL, it expands the range table into a series of explicit filter conditions:

SELECT * FROM ZCDS_MY_VIEW
WHERE KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE001'
   OR KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE002'
   OR KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE003'
   -- ... potentially thousands more conditions

If lr_range contains hundreds or thousands of entries - entirely realistic for document processing, purchase order workflows, or batch jobs - HANA must parse and compile this statement each time it is executed.
This compilation overhead, not the data retrieval itself, is what drives CPU spikes and degraded response times.

Why this goes undetected: In development and testing, range tables are typically small (a handful of test records).
The problem scales silently with production data volumes, only surfacing once the system is under real load.

This pattern is additionally harmful because:

  • It generates near-identical but technically distinct SQL statements (different values each run), preventing effective plan cache reuse.
  • It worsens over time as data volumes and document counts grow.

The Solution: FOR ALL ENTRIES with HANA FDA Hint

Note: FDA for FOR ALL ENTRIES is enabled by default on ABAP kernel >= 7.45 (dbs/hdb/prefer_join_with_fda = 1).
The hint &prefer_join_with_fda 1& in the examples below is a per-statement control - useful for targeted application or troubleshooting - not a feature activation.
If you are on a modern kernel, the mechanism is already active system-wide.

Replace the IN operator with FOR ALL ENTRIES IN, combined with a HANA Fast Data Access (FDA) hint:

SELECT *
  FROM zcds_my_view
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
  FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @LR_range
  WHERE key_field = @LR_range-low
  %_HINTS HDB '&prefer_join_with_fda 1&'.

Why This Works: SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)

FDA is a SAP HANA optimization specifically designed for FOR ALL ENTRIES queries. Instead of expanding the input list into SQL filter conditions, HANA:

  1. Loads the input list into a HANA-managed temporary table in memory.
  2. Joins the CDS view result against this temporary table.
  3. Lets the HANA query optimizer determine the most efficient join strategy.

The result is a single, stable, reusable SQL execution plan regardless of how many entries are in the input list.
Compilation overhead is eliminated, plan cache reuse is maximized, and CPU consumption is substantially reduced.

Comparison: IN vs FOR ALL ENTRIES + FDA

AspectIN '@LR_range'FOR ALL ENTRIES + FDA
SQL generatedOne condition per range entrySingle join against temp table
Compile timeGrows with range sizeConstant
Plan cache reuseLow (unique SQL per execution)High (stable plan)
CPU impact at scaleHighLow
ABAP complexityMinimalSlightly higher

 

Important considerations for FOR ALL ENTRIES: If the input table is empty, FOR ALL ENTRIES returns no rows, unlike IN which would return all rows in that case.
It also implicitly removes duplicate rows from the result set.
Ensure your application logic accounts for both behaviors before applying this change.
 

General Best Practices

Always Test in a Non-Production System First

Both fixes involve changes at the database or ABAP layer that affect execution plans.
Apply in a development or quality system first, execute the affected Fiori apps or programs, verify correct results, and confirm the execution plan has changed as expected via M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE before promoting to production.

Manage the SQL Plan Cache Proactively

After applying any execution hint or changing ABAP logic, clear the relevant SQL Plan Cache entries.
Leaving stale entries in place will delay the effect of any optimization until the cache entry naturally expires.

-- Clear cache entries for a specific CDS view
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

-- Clear cache entries for a specific statement hash
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_HASH = 'your_statement_hash_here';

Monitor Regularly, Not Just Reactively

Set up routine monitoring for:

  • CPU usage trends over 7-day rolling windows - distinguishes growing load from isolated incidents.
  • Top SQL statements by CPU time via M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE or HANA Cockpit's SQL Monitor.
  • Out-of-Memory dumps - a leading indicator of memory pressure before they become business-impacting outages.

Early detection allows you to address performance issues during planned maintenance windows rather than under incident pressure.

Summary

Both issues share a common theme: code that looks correct and performs acceptably in development causes measurable production impact at scale.
The fixes are targeted, low-risk, and reversible.

IssueRoot CauseSolutionSAP Note
High CPU on CDS views with localeWITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) prevents HEX engine usageEnable hex_enable_with_parameters_locale (prerequisite), then add USE_HEX_PLAN via @consumption.dbHints (preferred) or ANNOTATION fallback

3513507, 3076534,
2717265

High CPU from large filter listsABAP IN operator generates thousands of SQL conditionsUse FOR ALL ENTRIES with FDA hint2399993

 

References

  • SAP Note 3513507 - HANA HEX Engine not used for queries containing WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?)
  • SAP Note 3076534 - Temporary use of ANNOTATION HINT
  • SAP Note 2399993 - FAQ: SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)
  • SAP Note 2717265 - ABAP CDS: Using Database Hints via Annotations (preferred approach for custom CDS views)
  • SAP Help Portal — Accessing Localized Texts in CDS Views
  • SAP HANA Administration Guide — SQL Plan Cache

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA System Conversion: Reduce the memory footprint of Finance obsolete tables
entry.content:
When converting to SAP S/4HANA, Finance teams often expect an immediate reduction in database size—but this doesn’t always happen. After the System Conversion, the system still contains obsolete ECC finance data and legacy tables, which means the overall data footprint may remain unchanged at first. To take full advantage of SAP S/4HANA’s simplified finance data model, it’s important to perform a proper post‑conversion clean up.

SAP recommends removing content from obsolete tables with notes:

SAP Note 2190137 SAP S/4HANA Finance: Reduce memory footprint of obsolete tables. SAP Note  2569435  MM-IM: Save DB memory and remove data that is not covered by Information Lifecycle Management.

Customers must not only implement the relevant SAP Notes but also complete any manual corrections or follow-up activities. These extra steps are often essential for fully realizing the desired results, including data cleanup, system optimization, and maintaining performance improvements.

Additionally, the Finance and Basis teams should identify any remaining outdated data after conversion. SAP Note 2661837 provides guidance on preventing legacy structures from occupying unnecessary database space. Once cleanup is complete, running the SAP Note 1872170 is crucial to reassess the data footprint, which supports more accurate capacity planning and cost management.

From a Finance perspective, this cleanup is a key step—not just a technical task. Reducing unnecessary data improves system performance, supports faster reporting and financial close processes, and helps control infrastructure costs. A successful S/4HANA conversion doesn’t end with go‑live; cleaning up obsolete Finance data is what unlocks the real value of the move.

Conclusion

In summary, a successful SAP S/4HANA Finance conversion involves more than just completing the technical migration and going live. While the database may not immediately shrink after the upgrade, real advantages come from cleaning up outdated Finance data and legacy tables. By following SAP’s recommended notes and collaborating with the Finance and Basis teams, organizations can eliminate unnecessary data, enhance system performance, and gain clearer insights into their actual data volume.

Please “like” if you found this blog post interesting!  

Please add in the comment section any additional topics you think are valuable to have included in this and future blog posts.

– Brought to you by the S/4HANA RIG

entry.title: EDI in the age of agentic AI, the structural mismatch nobody is naming
entry.content:
Somewhere in most people’s houses, there is a road atlas. Beautifully printed, comprehensive, the place you were looking for always on the join between two pages or on the fold where the binding had eaten the postcode. Then the internet arrived and you printed out a route from a website, which was a digital map in name and a dead letter on the passenger seat. The thing that actually changed driving was location services. The moment the map knew where you were and where the closures were, it stopped being an artefact and became a network you could query. Then Waze went further: routing you using information contributed by every other driver on the road. A car on the hard shoulder. A speed camera. A tailback the official feed would not catch for twenty minutes. The map was no longer a thing you read. It was a network where every participant made the whole more intelligent for everyone else.

So, time to drag this back to my actual day job, which is business networks and S/4HANA. Every S/4 programme has to answer a deceptively simple question: how will this enterprise communicate with the outside world once the transformation is done? The old plumbing breaks. IDocs are restructured or retired, partner mappings need re-testing, and on-premises middleware that has quietly carried the business for fifteen years, invisible until the moment it failed, cannot keep up. The bill comes due. But the real choice is not how to fix the plumbing. It is whether the connectivity layer should remain a way of moving documents between two known parties, or become a queryable network that an autonomous agent can reason over. The answer the market is offering, namely modernise the EDI, move it to the cloud, add a bit of AI, is the corporate equivalent of printing out a route from the website. Technically more modern. Functionally the same piece of paper.

Give cloud EDI its due first. Moving an on-premises EDI estate into a managed cloud genuinely helps: better resilience, faster partner onboarding, centralised monitoring, and no more single-point-of-failure when your one EDI specialist retires. I am not arguing against any of it. I am arguing that none of it changes what the architecture is.

Cloud EDI is still EDI. It is still bilateral. It is still static. It produces a dead document, accurate at the moment of transmission, useless the moment anything changes, and it has nothing useful to offer an autonomous agent. Agents do not just process documents after the fact. They need to interrogate live, networked data, who has capacity now, at what price, under what constraints, and EDI has no way of exposing that.

This is the part that needs spelling out. Consider a worked example. A buyer agent at a Tier 1 manufacturer receives an order for three thousand units of a sub-assembly. To place that order well, the agent needs to know which suppliers across the network have capacity, at what price, with what lead time, tariff exposure, and carbon intensity. It needs to consider whether splitting the order across two suppliers reduces risk enough to justify the higher unit price, and whether either supplier is constrained by a Tier 2 component that has gone short this week. I have sat through several of those demos this year. Not one of them could have been accomplished over a one-to-one document pipe. Not one. The agent needs a network where it can ask a question and get an answer that reflects the current state of that network.

The architectural requirement is the same whatever you call it: networked, queryable, shared state across thousands of partners, with event-level visibility and the means to act. SAP Business Network is the specific instantiation of this within the S/4HANA ecosystem. The label matters less than the property. EDI was engineered in the 1970s to do the opposite of all of it.

Watch the market on this one. Read any vendor argument for "modernising your EDI to the cloud" carefully and you will find them describing a system that decouples mapping from the ERP, holds partner profiles centrally, and offers a real-time cockpit across thousands of partners. That is not modernised EDI. That is a network with a different name on the door. The market is quietly building the right thing while loudly selling the wrong word for it.

Two numbers tell you the shape of it. Interos, in its 6 Supply Chain Risks for 2026 report, finds that 90% of organisations remain blind to sub-tier disruptions for up to 48 hours. APQC's 2026 Supply Chain Priorities and Challenges: Cross-Industry Report finds that only one in three organisations have a strategy this year to increase visibility into lower-tier suppliers. Two-thirds are not even planning to do anything about it. One-to-one connections cannot reach those lower tiers. They cannot reach an agentic workflow either.

You already know I work in this space. The companies already on a network are building compound advantage in supplier visibility, agentic readiness, and network intelligence that a one-to-one pipe cannot replicate after the fact.

This should not be a Phase 2 conversation. It should not be kicked to 2028. It should not be left for the next team to discover. By then the programme team has moved on. The decision is business as usual. Nobody around the table remembers it was once a choice. A one-to-one pipe cannot reroute when a supplier fails, recompute when a tariff lands, or answer when an agent asks it a question. A network can do all three. The companies that defer will spend the next three years falling behind quietly, and in an agentic world three years is no longer a planning horizon. It is a generation. Kicking this to 2028 is not a deferral. It is a decision to fall behind, taken by people who will not be there to see it land.

The road atlas works. So does the printed page from the route planner. Neither of them is going to tell you about the lorry on the hard shoulder at the next junction, or that a faster route has just opened up that the rest of your competitors are already taking.

entry.title: What a Document AI Workshop Taught Me About Clean Core
entry.content:
There is something I enjoy almost as much as writing content about SAP, and that is reading what people have to say in popular discussion forums regarding topics that may be seen as rather controversial. I think that many times, the fact that some themes generate so much discussion has more to do with people not really understanding them than with the proper complexity of the product, strategy or idea.

In the last couple of weeks, I read a lot of discussion around Clean Core, both in SAP Community and in these online forums. I was amazed by how people defined the concept. I mean, you could tell that the opinion they were communicating was, of course, directly linked to the way they each define Clean Core, and that is exactly what surprised me: everyone defined it differently. Some described Clean Core as a way to reduce customization, others associated it with cloud ERP, and a few questioned whether it was even realistic in large SAP environments. 

This reading coincided with the preparation and hosting of our hands-on SAP Document AI workshop together with SAP, although at first I didn’t see any connection between the discussions about Clean Core and the hackathon, by the end of the workshop, I found myself thinking about Clean Core in a way I hadn't before. 

In this case, our workshop was focused on a particular scenario involving document processing. Prior to the event, we had already created a Proof of Concept for one of our customers, who was working with a huge number of documents related to their operations from various regions and business units. The problem lay in the fact that it was not just about data extraction from invoices and tickets. Rather, the issue here was the high percentage of manual labor required for proper document classification, validation, and linking every file to the right business process. During our preparation for the presentation, we paid most of our attention to AI possibilities provided by SAP Document AI.

The unexpected aspect of the workshop discussions turned out to be the shift of focus to an area completely unrelated to what was being discussed in the first place. Naturally, people did show interest in the accuracy of document extraction, and in capabilities of AI as well. However, most of the questions were related to integration and scalability, how the solution could work in participants' environments, how well it would integrate with other applications, and how it would help them expand their capabilities without disrupting existing processes. 

Looking back, this is probably where the connection with Clean Core started to become clearer for me. Throughout the workshop, nobody was asking how to modify SAP so that Document AI could work. Nobody was discussing new custom tables, custom transactions, or large changes to core ERP processes. Several participants were already thinking about additional use cases, while others wanted to understand how the approach could scale across different document types and business units. The most prominent questions were adoption, integration, operational impact, and long-term scalability. The conversations focused on how to introduce a new capability while keeping the existing landscape stable.

That realization made me think about many of the online discussions I had been reading. A large number of them seemed to describe Clean Core primarily in terms of restrictions. The conversation often revolved around what organizations should avoid doing, what should not be customized, or what should be moved outside the ERP. While those aspects are certainly part of the concept, the workshop made me wonder if that perspective is too narrow.

What I saw during the event was not a limitation on innovation. If anything, it was the opposite. SAP Document AI was introducing completely new capabilities into existing business processes. The innovation was real, the business value was clear, and yet none of it required redesigning the ERP core itself. For the first time, I started thinking about Clean Core not as a strategy for reducing customization, but as a strategy for deciding where innovation should happen.

The more I thought about it after the workshop, the more I understood why so many people define Clean Core differently. Some people approach it from an architecture perspective, others from governance, and others from the realities of cloud ERP. Not one of these definitions can be wrong per se. But I found myself asking whether it is because of the lack of time spent on analyzing what the tool actually does for us and allows us to do.

In retrospect, what really got through to me at the workshop was not the demo as such. We had worked on getting ready the Proof of Concept, exercises and scenarios that would be demonstrated at the workshop during weeks in advance. What stayed with me after the workshop were the discussions triggered by all these. The most engaging ones did not concern the changes in ERP but rather organization of the processes that allowed introducing new functionality and retaining flexibility in the process. As much as I am not sure yet that there is such a definition of Clean Core accepted by all, after reading multiple threads online I am only even surer about this.

For me, Clean Core is no longer just about keeping systems standard. It is about creating the conditions for innovation to happen without making future change more difficult than it needs to be. Perhaps that is why I now see Clean Core less as a technical principle and more as a question every organization eventually has to answer: where should new capabilities live?

entry.title: Handling Large Volumes of Data Between SAP CPI and S/4 HANA
entry.content:
Most SAP CPI integrations work perfectly in development. Then you hit production — and everything changes.

500,000 sales orders. 2 million material records. Suddenly you're seeing timeouts, memory errors, and an S/4 HANA system that slows down for every other user. I've been there. Here's what actually works.

The Real Problem Is End-to-End, Not Just CPI

High-volume data processing touches four layers simultaneously — the ABAP database, the OData gateway, the Cloud Connector tunnel, and the CPI runtime. Most teams focus only on CPI and wonder why tuning iFlow settings doesn't fix anything. The answer is usually one layer below.

Layer 1 — Fix S/4 HANA First

Before writing a single iFlow step, go to your S/4 HANA system and check one thing: are your filter fields indexed?

When CPI filters OData results by LastChangeDateTime, the ABAP layer runs a SQL WHERE clause against the underlying table. Without a database index on that field, the database scans every single row — all 10 million of them — just to return 500 records.

That's the difference between a 0.3-second query and a 15-second one. Across 2,000 pages of data, that's 10 minutes versus 8 hours — while blocking ABAP work processes for every other user on the system.

Check indexes in SE11 → table name → Indexes tab. If your filter field isn't there, raise it with your BASIS team before anything else.

Also verify:

  • OData service is active in SICF and /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE
  • A dedicated technical user exists with the correct S_SERVICE role
  • Test the full OData call with $top=500 in /IWFND/GW_CLIENT before building

Layer 2 — Tune the Cloud Connector

The default connection pool in SAP Cloud Connector is 5 connections. For high-volume integration, that's a bottleneck you can't see — requests queue silently and your iFlow slows without throwing errors.

Increase the pool to 20–50 connections. Enable HTTP Keep-Alive. Enable GZIP compression on the system mapping — this reduces OData payload size by 60–70% and takes load off the tunnel significantly.

Layer 3 — Stop Fighting the OData Adapter

Here's the mistake I see most often: developers manually add $top=500&$skip=0 to the query string, then also enable Process in Pages in the OData adapter. The adapter adds its own pagination on top — values conflict, and you get duplicated pages or an infinite loop on page 1.

The CPI OData Receiver Adapter handles pagination natively. Enable Process in Pages, set your page size (200–500 records), and put only this in your query options:

  • $select — fetch only the fields you need (cuts payload by 60–80%)
  • $filter — for delta loading by date or key field

That's it. No $top. No $skip. Let the adapter do its job.

The Delta Load Pattern — Non-Negotiable at Scale

Never run full loads on recurring integrations with more than 10,000 records. Instead:

  1. Save the run timestamp to a CPI DataStore after each successful execution
  2. On the next run, filter with $filter=LastChangeDateTime gt datetime'<saved timestamp>'
  3. Process only what changed — update the timestamp on success

This turns millions of records into hundreds per run.

The Pattern That Holds at Production Scale

Scheduler

  → Read timestamp from DataStore

  → OData Adapter (Process in Pages ON)

  → Splitter (one record per message)

  → Process + write to target

  → Save new timestamp to DataStore

Simple. Resilient. Scalable.

Here is the skeletal working flow between S/4 OnPrem to CPI to pull data using Get.

  • Timer: OnDeployment
  • Content Modifier4:

Use timestamp variable to fetch data based on specific specific date

property timestamp to be given based on the abap field timestamp like(20000203062111)

 

 

  • Add looping process call
  1. Expression Type : Non XML
  2. Condition Expression: use ${property.Receiver1.OData.hasMoreRecords} is a CPI-internal exchange property that the OData Receiver Adapter sets automatically after each page call.
  3. Max. Numbers of Iterations: 10 (depends on the payload use this effectively, if you expect to receive 1lakh record use 100 to 120)
  4. Action When Max Iterations Reached: Throw Exception

 

 

 

  • add Request/Reply pallet to connect ODATA receiver adapter.
  • use ODATA V2 receiver Adapter

 

 

  1. For my testing using Page Size as 30
  2. Process in pages : checked

In the query there is no need of using $TOP and $SKIP, just make sure these are enabled in ABAP end that’s it,

CPI will take care in calling the looping process call until
Receiver1.OData.hasMoreRecords = ‘false’

 

 

 

 

  • Add ContentModifier2

Every new OData call replaces in.body completely. By the time the loop ends, you only have the last page — all previous pages are lost.

You are accumulating each page response into a property before the next OData call overwrites in.body.

Property name: appendPayload

SourceType: Expression
SourceValue: ${property.appendPayload}${in.body}

 

 

  • Add Content Modifier 3
  1. Under Main integration process add the property appendPayload to the Message body as ${property.appendPayload} to send it to target.
  2. Use all the transformational pallet functions under the Local Integration Process where ODATA is calling to S/4, so that pallet functions process data page wise, at the end you can append payload using the below approach.

 

Lets deploy and monitor

  • CPI Helper

 

  1. CamelLoopIndex : 3

is a built-in CPI property that tells you which iteration the loop is currently on.

  1. For the last looping index got Receiver1.OData.hasMoreRecords = false, next loop will end the process.
  2. SkipRecords_Receiver1.OData: is the property CPI uses internally to track and inject the $skip value into every OData page request — it increments automatically so you never need to calculate it yourself.

 

 

SkipRecords_Receiver1.OData

 

High-volume SAP CPI integration isn't complicated once you understand each layer's role. The teams that struggle are the ones treating it as a black box — tuning CPI while a missing database index silently kills performance.

Know your stack. Test each layer. Let the tools do what they're designed to do.

Have you faced high-volume integration challenges in your SAP landscape? Drop a comment — I'd love to hear your approach.

#SAP #SAPCPI #S4HANA #CloudIntegration #SAPIntegration #OData #CloudConnector #SAPDeveloper #IntegrationArchitecture

entry.title: Extending CKM3N in S/4HANA: A Practical Approach for Multi-Material and Multi-Period Analysis
entry.content:
The CKM3N Limitation

Anyone who has worked with CKM3N in S/4HANA knows how valuable it is for Material Ledger analysis. However, when it comes to analyzing multiple materials or comparing different periods, the standard approach quickly becomes restrictive.

This limitation motivated me to explore a more analytical approach for Material Ledger consumption using CDS Views.

CKM3N Standard

Why This Becomes a Problem

In Product Costing and Material Ledger scenarios, it is very common to require comparative analysis across multiple materials and periods.

With CKM3N, this often means executing the transaction repeatedly, exporting data, and manually consolidating information.

This process can become time-consuming and makes it harder to identify cost trends efficiently.

A Different Approach Using CDS Views

The approach was to model Material Ledger data using CDS Views, preserving a structure similar to CKM3N while enabling collective analysis.

The objective was not to replace CKM3N, but to complement it with broader analytical capabilities.

  • Multiple materials
  • Multiple periods
  • Hierarchical visualization
  • Analytical consumption through different frontends

Multi-material Selection

Performance Considerations

Performance became a critical aspect due to the high volume of Material Ledger data.

To improve scalability, filter pushdown strategies and optimized CDS modeling were applied to ensure that filters were executed as early as possible.

This was particularly important when working with Material Ledger datasets and cost component split information.

Execution / Performance

Results

Although the current implementation was explored using an ALV-based visualization, the CDS-based model also enables alternative consumption patterns, such as Fiori applications or API exposure.
 
This flexibility allows the same analytical foundation to be reused across different scenarios depending on business needs.

ALV Table

Tree View

 To improve the analytical experience, a lightweight header inspired by Fiori design principles was introduced in the ALV output, displaying key indicators at runtime.

 
This allows users to quickly identify relevant insights without navigating through detailed line items, bringing a more modern and efficient interaction model even in a classic ALV context.

Final Thoughts

This exercise highlighted how analytical flexibility can significantly improve Material Ledger consumption in S/4HANA.

I am curious to understand how other teams are addressing similar CKM3N limitations.

👉Feel free to share your approach or connect to exchange ideas.

entry.title: SAP ABAP Data Dictionary :A Complete Beginner's Guide to Domains, Data Elements, and Database Tables
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP ABAP development, one of the most fundamental and frequently used components is the Data Dictionary (DDIC). Whether you are creating reports, SmartForms, ALV reports, interfaces, or custom applications, DDIC plays a critical role in defining and managing data consistently across the SAP landscape.

The SAP Data Dictionary serves as a centralized repository that stores metadata definitions for database objects. It helps developers maintain data integrity, improve reusability, and ensure consistency throughout the system.

This article provides a comprehensive overview of SAP Data Dictionary concepts, including Domains, Data Elements, Database Tables, Structures, Table Types, and Technical Settings. By the end of this guide, you will have a solid understanding of DDIC and its importance in SAP ABAP development.

What is SAP Data Dictionary (DDIC)?

SAP Data Dictionary (DDIC) is a central repository where all data definitions and metadata are maintained.

It acts as a bridge between SAP applications and the underlying database by providing standardized definitions for business data.

Key Benefits of DDIC

✔ Centralized Data Management

✔ Data Consistency Across Applications

✔ Reusability of Data Objects

✔ Improved Performance

✔ Reduced Development Effort

✔ Database Independence

Execute T-code or Path

From the SAP Easy Access Screen: execute transaction code SE11 or follow the SAP menu path and Execute: SE11 ABAP Dictionary

Transaction codeSE11
Menu PathSAP Menu > Tools > ABAP Workbench > Development > SE11 – ABAP Dictionary

 

DDIC Architecture Overview

The relationship between DDIC objects follows a hierarchical structure:

Domain
   ↓
Data Element
   ↓
Table Field
   ↓
Database Table

 

Domain & How to create a Domain?

A Domain defines the technical attributes of a field.

It specifies:

  • Data Type
  • Length
  • Decimal Places
  • Value Range
  • Fixed Values

Think of a Domain as a blueprint that determines how data should be stored in the database.

  • Choose the Domain radio button
  • Give the domain name that you want to create.

Enter the short description of the domain. You can maintain text up to 60 characters.

On domain definition, you need to maintain the format details of the data type and length.

After providing all the required details for the domain, click on the save button.

Select the local object to save the created domain in an object directory.

Check Syntax & Activate.

Click Activate (Ctrl+F3) to activate the Domain.

Advantages of Domains

  • Eliminates redundant definitions
  • Improves consistency
  • Easy maintenance
  • Reusable across multiple applications

Data Element & How to create a Data Element?

A Data Element provides business meaning to a field.

While a Domain defines technical properties, a Data Element explains what the field represents from a business perspective.

Data Element Contains

  • Field Labels
  • Documentation
  • Business Description
  • Domain Reference

The Domain controls storage and validation, while the Data Element tells users that the field represents Employee Status.

  • Choose the Data Element radio button.
  • Enter the Data Element name that you want to create.

As we are creating data element, select data element radio button and press enter.

  1. Update the short description of the Data Element.
  2. Select the predefined radio button and update the data type, and length fields.

Text information is updated in field label tab, click on field label and update the following information.

  •  Click on the save button (ctrl+s) to save the configured data element in a package. You get a message as “ZDATA_ELEMENT_EMP saved.
  • Click Check (Ctrl+F2) and then Activate (Ctrl+F3).

Domain vs Data Element

DomainData Element
Technical DefinitionBusiness Definition
Data Type & LengthLabels & Documentation
Validation RulesUser Understanding
ReusableReusable

Database Tables & How to create a table?

Database Tables are used to store application data physically in the database.

Every business transaction ultimately stores information in one or more database tables.

Components of a Table

  • Fields
  • Primary Key
  • Technical Settings
  • Delivery Class
  • Foreign Keys

On the initial screen, select the database table option and enter the name of the database table that you want to be created. After updating name click on create button. Here we are creating ZTEST_EMP table.

On the next screen, update the following data. By default delivery and maintenance tab is selected.

  1. Short Description: Update the short description of the database table.
  2. Delivery Class: Update the delivery class from the list, you can get the list be clicking the search option (F4).
  3. Data Browser/ Table View Maintain: Select the display/maintenance allowed from the drop down list.

Select field tab and update the following data.

  1. Field: Update the field name in the field column (16 characters)
  2. Key: Select the key option if you want the field as table key.
  3. Data Element: Update the data element key from the list.

Click on save button to save the configured data. You get a message as ZTEST_EMP saved.

Types of SAP Tables

1. Transparent Tables

Most commonly used table type.

Characteristics:

  • One SAP table corresponds to one database table.
  • Data is physically stored.

Examples:

MARA
KNA1
LFA1

2. Pooled Tables

Used to store small amounts of data.

Multiple logical tables share one physical database table.

3. Cluster Tables

Store related records together.

Primarily used in older SAP systems.

Note:

In SAP S/4HANA, Transparent Tables are predominantly used.

 

Key Takeaways

• Domain defines technical properties.
• Data Element provides business meaning.
• Database Tables store application data physically.
• DDIC ensures consistency and reusability across SAP applications.

 

Conclusion

SAP Data Dictionary (DDIC) serves as the foundation of SAP ABAP development by providing centralized definitions for business data. Understanding Domains, Data Elements, and Database Tables is essential for building scalable and maintainable SAP applications.

A strong understanding of DDIC not only helps developers create efficient solutions but also plays a significant role in SAP ABAP interviews and real-world project implementations.

**In the next article, we will explore Structures, Table Types, and Technical Settings in detail.**

entry.title: SAP SUM Reset: When, Why, and How to Reset SUM
entry.content:
Hello Reader,

Let's be real — if you're reading this, things probably haven't gone according to plan. Your SUM run is stuck somewhere in the Execution phase, your client wants answers, and the clock is ticking.
You're not alone. This is one of the most common and stressful situations a BASIS administrator can face.

This guide will walk you through everything you need to know — not just the commands, but the logic behind them — so you can make confident decisions under pressure.

Moreover, this would be a Quick Answers to What You're Probably Googling Right Now:

  • Can I reset SUM right now?
  • How to reset the SUM?
  • What happens if I just restore the database?
  • What if I've already deleted the SUM directory?

What Does a SUM Reset Actually Do?

A SUM reset is designed to bring the system back to a state where the upgrade can be restarted.

Depending on the phase and scenario, SUM can:

  • Reverse upgrade activities
  • Remove upgrade artifacts
  • Clean up the shadow instance
  • Restore upgrade consistency

A reset is different from:

  • Stopping SUM
  • Deleting the SUM directory
  • Restoring only the database

When Should You Consider a SUM Reset?

A SUM reset is typically considered in the following situations:

  1. Upgrade Stuck in Execution Phase
    You have spent considerable time analysing logs and retrying phases, but the upgrade is still blocked.
  2. Downtime Window Is Running Out
    Business requirements may force you to abandon the upgrade and return the system to service.
  3. Upgrade State Has Become Inconsistent
    Repeated failures, corrupted upgrade metadata, or unexpected SUM behaviour may require a complete restart.
  4. Customer Requests Rollback
    Sometimes the safest option is to stop the upgrade and plan another attempt later.

When Is a Reset Actually Possible?

This is one of the most important things to understand before you do anything. SUM's ability to reset depends on two conditions:

Condition

Can You Reset?

Notes

Cleanup has NOT been run

Yes

This is the normal rollback path

Cleanup HAS been run (after success)

No

You must use a pre-upgrade system backup

You chose Reset, reset completed

Cleanup is now mandatory

You must run cleanup after a successful reset

SUM directory was deleted manually

Partially

Re-extract SAR file, then follow the stuck-phase procedure

Important: Never run the Cleanup option from SUM's More menu unless you are 100% sure you won't need to roll back. Once cleanup runs, the only way back is a full system restore from a backup taken before the upgrade began.

 

Why Restoring the Database Is Not Enough

One of the most overlooked aspects of SAP upgrades is the shadow instance.

During an upgrade, SUM creates a shadow system that consumes:

  • CPU
  • Memory
  • Disk
  • SAP resources

If you only restore the database:

✓ Database returns to its previous state

X Shadow instance may still exist

X Upgrade artifacts may remain

X Future upgrades may encounter issues

This is why a proper SUM reset is often necessary after restoring the database.

 

Best Practice Before Entering Execution Phase

Before any major upgrade activity begins, make sure you have:

Full Database Backup

This is your primary recovery mechanism.

Backup of the SUM Directory

I strongly recommend taking a backup of the complete SUM directory at the end of preprocessing.

If a rollback becomes necessary later, this backup can save a significant amount of time.

 

How to Reset SUM Correctly

Scenario 1: SUM Reset Option Is Available

This is the easiest situation.

From the SUM UI: More → Reset

Allow SUM to complete the reset process.

After completion:

  • Upgrade changes are reversed
  • Shadow instance is removed
  • Upgrade state is cleaned up

Scenario 2: Upgrade Is Already Deep Into Execution

If rollback is required:

Step 1: Restore and recover the database.

Step 2: Restore the backed-up SUM directory.

Step 3: Start SUM using the restored backup.

Step 4: Execute the reset.

This ensures both the database and SUM state are returned to a consistent condition.

Manual Reset Procedure

In certain situations, you may need to perform a manual reset preparation.

Navigate to:

SUM/abap/bin

Execute:

SAPup reset prepare

Afterward:

  1. Stop all SAPup processes at OS level.
  2. Delete the SUM directory.
  3. Extract a fresh SUM package if needed.
  4. Start again from the beginning.

Common Mistake #1: Deleting the SUM Directory

Many administrators believe that deleting the SUM folder automatically resets the upgrade.

It does not.

SUM stores upgrade information inside the SAP system database.

When a new SUM package is extracted, it often reads the existing upgrade state and returns to the same phase.

Always perform a proper reset procedure rather than simply deleting SUM.

Common Mistake #2: Running Cleanup Too Early

After an update, SUM provides:

More → Cleanup

Be careful.

Once Cleanup has been executed:

  • Reset is no longer available
  • Rollback capability is removed

At that point, database restoration may be the only recovery option.

 

What If SUM Keeps Dropping Back into the Same Phase?

This happens when the Execution phase ran far enough to make changes to the system's upgrade versioning tables (UVERS), but not far enough to complete cleanly. Even after you clear the SUM directory and re-extract the SAR file, SUM reads those database tables and drops straight back into the last active phase.

This is not a bug. SUM is doing exactly what it's designed to do — resuming where it left off. The problem is that the state it's resuming from is inconsistent.

Ex: SAP Note 1790486 covers the scenario where SAP_ABA (or another software component) is in an undefined upgrade state that is not safe to proceed with.

In emergency situations, SAP may recommend running report RSUPGRES:

Important considerations:

  • Execute with DDIC
  • Use client 000
  • Run in background
  • Do not cancel the job manually

The report is intended to repair upgrade status inconsistencies and should be used carefully.

Note: RSUPGRES is a powerful and potentially destructive tool. Use it only as a last resort and follow every instruction. Do not improvise.

Afterward, many administrators also execute:

/$SYNC

and

/$TAB

to refresh buffers and synchronize system status.

 

A Word on Cleanup — Don't Confuse It with Reset

SUM has two end-of-process options that BASIS admins sometimes confuse:

Option

When It Appears

What It Does

Can You Reset After?

Reset

During the update process

Rolls back the upgrade, dissolves the shadow instance

Yes — but cleanup becomes mandatory afterward

Cleanup

After a successful update OR after a Reset

Removes SUM working files and state

No — reset is no longer possible after this

Note: Cleanup is a one-way door. Once you walk through it, you cannot come back without a full system restore. Always think twice before clicking it.

 

Key Takeaways

Most BASIS admins who've gone through a reset once develop a pre-Execution checklist they follow religiously. Here's what that looks like:

Before every upgrade:

✓ Take a full database backup

✓ Take a backup of the SUM directory

✓ Understand your rollback strategy

When an upgrade fails:

✓ Determine whether a SUM reset is still possible

✓ Do not immediately delete the SUM directory

✓ Consider the shadow instance impact

✓ Restore both the database and SUM state when necessary

Always remember that a successful rollback is not just about restoring the database. A complete recovery requires restoring upgrade consistency and ensuring the shadow instance is properly removed.

 

Have you run into a reset scenario that isn't covered here?

Drop it in the comments.
